text: The surgical field shows the corneal structures already dissected away, with the bladder dissection about to commence.
The uterine artery and uterine vein are visible running along the lateral side of the cervix.
The surgeon observes the urinary bladder adherent to the lower uterine segment, forming a visible band of scar tissue.
The surgeon identifies the correct plane of dissection below the scarred area, where the bladder is not adherent, facilitating easier dissection.
The surgeon begins the dissection process, focusing on the lateral window to safely separate the bladder from the lower uterine segment, avoiding the central scarred area.
The surgeon begins by separating the anterior and posterior leaf of the broad ligament, preparing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully performs dissection, aiming to maintain an avascular field to prevent bleeding that could obscure the view of the loose areolar tissue necessary for proper dissection.
The surgeon incises the uterovesical (UV) fold slightly below the layer of scar tissue, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon gently dissects the loose areolar tissue away from the lateral window, positioned just below the UV fold and above the area of the uterine artery.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection in the area, gradually revealing the uterine artery as the first anatomical landmark.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery as the first landmark, revealing the bare surface of the anterior cervix, before proceeding with further dissection.
The uterine artery is clearly visible, serving as the first landmark for proceeding with the dissection in the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the UV fold and makes small, gentle cuts close to the uterus to separate the UV fold after identifying the uterine artery.
The surgeon assesses the UV fold, which appears loosely attached, and proceeds to dissect it entirely before continuing to explore the surrounding area.
The surgeon assesses the UV fold and scar tissue, noting that they appear inseparable. The area is developed beforehand to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection of the scar tissue at its junction with the uterus, following the identification of the uterine artery and the anterior cervix as landmarks.
The bare cervix is visible, indicating that the surgical field is positioned high enough to minimize the risk of encountering the bladder during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the plane between the UV fold of peritoneum and the bladder, making an incision in the UV fold to enhance the visibility of the bladder.
The surgeon applies additional stretch on the uterus from the vaginal end and proceeds with the dissection of the UV fold, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon marks the area of scar tissue and carefully dissects above it to separate the bladder from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon incises the clear UV fold of peritoneum from the left to the right side, extending the incision up to the round ligament on the right, revealing the underlying bladder.
With the UV fold dissected down, the surgeon now has a clearer view of the bladder fascia, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the UV fold, progressing from the left side to the right side, reaching up to the midline.
The surgeon cuts the UV fold, revealing underlying fat tissue, indicating proximity to the bladder. The right round ligament has already been severed.
The surgeon dissects the bladder in a plane superior to the visible fat, ensuring the fat remains attached to the bladder during the procedure.

The surgeon attempts to develop the space on the right side, mirroring the dissection previously completed on the left side, with visible anatomical structures including the bladder fascia and UV fold.
The UV fold has been completely cut, and the surgeon returns to the left side to continue dissecting the bladder, focusing on separating the bladder from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses suction to remove excess blood from the surgical field, preventing staining of the loose areolar tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the bladder, focusing on maintaining the plane superior to the fat to ensure the fat remains attached to the bladder.
Traction is applied from the vaginal end, visibly tensing the bladder and separating it from the anterior surface of the cervix.
The surgeon gently lifts the bulk of the bladder, tenting it over the cervix to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon exposes the white surface of the cervix, with the uterine artery visibly marked in red, scar tissue marked in blue, and the correct plane of dissection indicated by an orange arrow.
The surgeon identifies the position of the bladder below the cervix level, noting that it is unlikely to encounter the bladder at the most cranial point of the scar, which is under tension.
The surgeon carefully incises the scar tissue at its junction with the uterus, using caution to avoid damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon proceeds to cut through the scar tissue on the anterior surface of the cervix, avoiding the bladder, which has already been visualized and dissected.

The surgeon continues cutting, revealing a slight color difference between the cervix and bladder. As the uterus is pushed upward by the manipulator, the bladder and cervix move separately, aiding in identifying the correct plane of dissection.
The surgeon maintains tension on the bladder with one hand while gently lifting it up to continue dissection near the cervix, carefully separating the bladder from adjacent scar tissue.
The surgeon continues dissecting scar tissue at the junction to the uterus, progressing towards the midline while ensuring ergonomic ease during the procedure.

The surgeon reaches the midline and begins operating from the opposite side, connecting the established plane of dissection to the plane on the other side, ensuring continuity in the surgical approach.

The surgeon uses a grasper to apply stretch on the bladder while simultaneously stretching the uterus with a uterine manipulator or myoma spiral, clearly delineating the boundary between the bladder and the lower uterine segment.
The surgeon shifts the uterus to the patient's left side, aiming to locate and create a lateral window on the patient's right side for further dissection.
The surgeon lifts the bladder and performs blunt dissection in the loose areolar tissue to locate the uterine artery.

The surgeon gently incises the loose posterior peritoneum near the lateral border of the cervix, revealing the uterine artery and vein. Small vessels are visible, with minimal bleeding observed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues tunneling dissection, aiming to locate the uterine artery while exposing the glistening anterior surface of the cervix.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery beneath the cervix, noting the distinct color difference of the cervix and the softer feel of the tissue where the artery is located.
The surgeon palpates the bladder, which appears soft, and the anterior surface of the cervix, which is visibly firm in consistency.
The surgeon uses a grasper to reveal the uterine artery below, while the bladder is visible above the grasper.
The surgeon carefully removes loose areolar tissue to expose the glistening anterior surface of the cervix, using a grasper for precise manipulation.
The surgeon positions the bladder on top and begins cutting close to the cervix, visibly pushing the bladder away from the cervix.
The assistant makes the bladder taut from the contralateral side, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the anatomical structures during the procedure.
The surgeon applies traction to the bladder, preparing to cut it close to the cervix, ensuring safety and reducing dependence on the assistant.
The surgeon, with the assistant providing traction, dissects anterior to the uterine artery to reveal the white glistening surface of the cervix.
The surgeon incises the bladder scar tissue near the cervix, carefully pushing the bladder down to maintain the correct plane of dissection.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel or bipolar vessel sealer and cutter to take small bites of tissue, effectively dissecting the bladder away from the uterus, both of which are under adequate stretch.

The surgeon identifies the correct plane of dissection, revealing the white cervix and uterine vessels running anteriorly. Loose areolar tissue is visible in the lateral space, confirming the targeted anatomical area.
The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the bladder and makes gentle cuts on the anterior surface of the cervix, carefully dissecting the tissue in this area.
With the lateral space clearly developed, the surgeon proceeds to cut the remaining scar tissue, revealing the correct plane of dissection from the patient's left side.
The surgeon connects the previously dissected planes and gently pushes the bladder downward, ensuring the anatomical structures are properly aligned.

The surgeon gently dissects the bladder in small increments, gradually moving it out to clear the surgical field. With both lateral planes developed, the uterus is pushed cranially and positioned in the midline to expose the tongue-shaped tissue of the bladder for further dissection.
The surgeon addresses the adhesion on the anterior surface of the cervix, using a grasper to manipulate the bladder and make precise cuts to separate the adherent tissue.
The surgeon confidently cuts the tongue-shaped tissue of the bladder, utilizing the correctly developed dissection plane from both sides.
The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to lift the bladder scar tissue, while making gentle cuts with the right-handed instrument to progressively push the bladder away from the scar tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates the concept of dissecting below the scar tissue, revealing the bladder adequately separated from both sides. The lateral window is visible, allowing safe continuation of the surgery.
The surgeon attempts to develop the plane of dissection by incising the posterior peritoneum, as an alternative approach when the anterior peritoneum cannot be effectively incised.
The surgeon incises the posterior peritoneum, revealing the uterine artery without the need for bladder dissection, demonstrating a clear view of the artery.
The surgeon gently lifts the posterior peritoneum, revealing underlying anatomical structures and creating a space for further dissection.
The surgeon cuts to expose the uterine vessels, which are clearly identified, allowing the procedure to continue.
The uterine artery is visible following the incision of the posterior peritoneum.
